context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly pretty lost on this climate action prospectus assignment. Can you just explain the whole thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you work through it! Before we dive in — have you already picked a case study city, and did you complete the SWOT Analysis mentioned in the assignment? That'll help me understand where you're starting from.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad explanation of the whole assignment, which risks me doing the conceptual work for them. Instead, I should use guided discovery to find out where they actually are in the process — what they've already done, what city they've chosen, etc. This way I can target support precisely and avoid over-scaffolding. I'll ask a focused question to get started.

exchanges.student: Yeah, kind of, but I’m not even sure what parts of the SWOT matter here anymore. I have a city, but now I feel confused about the whole assignment, like what each section is supposed to do.
exchanges.tutor: Let's focus. Looking at the assignment, what do you think the main deliverable actually is — what are you being asked to produce? Take a shot at describing it in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing general confusion rather than a specific question. I should help them break it down without explaining it all for them. The assignment has a clear structure — two components, each with five sections. Rather than explaining all of it, I'll ask the student to identify what they think the core task is, using guided discovery. This keeps the cognitive work on their side.

exchanges.student: Maybe two policy ideas? But then there are all these sections and I can’t tell what goes where. Could you just walk me through the whole structure really simply from start to finish?
exchanges.tutor: You're right that it's two policy components — good start. Rather than me walking through all of it, look at the five sections listed for each component. Which one confuses you the most? Let's tackle them one at a time.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track identifying 'two policy ideas' but wants me to walk them through the whole structure, which would be doing the intellectual work for them. Instead, I should affirm their partial understanding and push them to engage with the structure themselves by looking at the assignment. I'll ask them to pick one section they're most confused about rather than explaining all five sections.

exchanges.student: Honestly maybe all of them. Like I thought I got “policy recommendation,” but then I’m not sure how it’s different from “opportunity,” and now “interactions” is throwing me off too.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's a real question worth thinking about. Here's one to chew on: what's the difference between identifying a problem that *could* be addressed, versus actually saying what you'd *do* about it? How would you describe that difference in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has narrowed down to a real conceptual question — the difference between 'Opportunity' and 'Policy Recommendation,' and what 'Interactions' means. This is good progress. I should use Socratic questioning to help them think through the distinction themselves rather than explaining it. I'll focus on just the Opportunity vs. Policy Recommendation distinction first, as that's the more foundational confusion.

exchanges.student: I guess “opportunity” is like the opening or area where action could happen, and “policy recommendation” is the actual thing to do? But I still feel like I need the full picture or I’m going to mix everything up.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right — you've got that distinction well. Now apply the same thinking to 'Interactions.' What do you think it means for one climate policy to *interact* with another?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually nailed the Opportunity vs. Policy Recommendation distinction well. I should affirm that clearly and briefly, then redirect to the 'Interactions' confusion they mentioned, rather than giving them the 'full picture' they're asking for. Giving the full picture would be over-scaffolding. I'll push them to think through 'Interactions' themselves using the same approach.

exchanges.student: Maybe like how one policy helps another one? I’m still really shaky though — can you please just give me a simple overview of what each section is for, because I keep losing track.
exchanges.tutor: Look at what you've already figured out on your own: Opportunity = where action is possible, Policy Recommendation = what you'd actually do, Interactions = how policies support each other. That's three of five sections. What do you think 'Context' and 'EJ Implications' might be about, based on the assignment description?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to explain everything, but they've actually demonstrated good understanding of three of the five sections through our conversation. I should point that out to them rather than rewarding the request for a full overview. I'll push them to notice their own progress and then ask about the remaining sections they haven't addressed yet — Context and EJ Implications — to keep the discovery going.

exchanges.student: I think context is the current problem in the city, and EJ is who gets affected unfairly maybe? I’m just not confident at all, and I kind of need the assignment explained as one complete thing instead of in little pieces.
exchanges.tutor: You just described all five sections correctly — you have the full picture. Now let's make it concrete. What city are you working with, and what's one major climate vulnerability you identified in your SWOT?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly identified all five sections on their own through guided discovery. This is a significant milestone and I should affirm it clearly. However, they keep asking for a complete explanation, which I should decline while pointing out that they've already built the full picture themselves. Now is the right moment to shift from conceptual understanding to actual application — I should push them toward their city and SWOT to start drafting, rather than continuing to discuss the structure abstractly.

exchanges.student: It’s Boston, I think flooding and heat maybe, but I’m not sure which one I’m supposed to build the component around. That’s why I feel stuck, like I need to hear how the whole prospectus is supposed to come together.
exchanges.tutor: You don't need to pick the 'right' one — you need to pick one and build with it. Think about Boston specifically: which of those two vulnerabilities — flooding or heat — do you think hits vulnerable communities harder, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is deflecting back to asking for a full explanation again, but they've actually made real progress and now have enough to start working. Flooding and heat are both valid vulnerabilities for Boston. Rather than explaining the prospectus structure again, I should push them to make a decision and start applying their knowledge. The assignment says to focus on vulnerabilities, EJ implications, and overlaps — so I'll ask them to think about which vulnerability connects more to environmental justice in Boston, which will help them choose and move forward.

exchanges.student: Maybe heat, because not everyone has AC and some neighborhoods probably have more trees than others? But then flooding also seems huge, so now I’m confused about everything again.
exchanges.tutor: Good news — you actually need two components, so you can use both. Your heat reasoning is strong and shows real EJ thinking. So let's move: take your heat component and try writing a draft 'Context' section for it. What's the current situation in Boston around heat?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles between flooding and heat, which is a minor decision that shouldn't block progress. They've shown good instincts on heat and EJ. Per the rules, if a student is obsessing about a detail that doesn't substantially affect the assignment, I should acknowledge it and ask them to move on. The assignment requires TWO components anyway, so they can actually use both. I should point this out to redirect their energy toward drafting.

exchanges.student: I don’t know how detailed it’s supposed to be, and I’m worried I’ll put the wrong stuff in the wrong section. Could you just give me a super simple big-picture explanation of how the two components and all the sections fit together?
exchanges.tutor: We've covered the structure thoroughly and you've shown you understand it. At this point, more explanation won't unstick you — writing will. Give the 'Context' section your best shot, even if it's rough. What's happening with heat in Boston right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked for a full explanation of the assignment structure multiple times — this is clearly a redundant pattern. They have demonstrated understanding of all five sections and have enough to start writing. Per the rules, after repeated redundancy I should firmly redirect and ask them to attempt written content rather than continuing to discuss structure. I should be direct and honest that continuing to explain won't help them — writing will.
